caseStudies.title: Headless Commerce Migration
caseStudies.description: Decoupling frontend and commerce engine for a global retailer.
caseStudies.overview: A global fashion retailer needed to migrate from a monolithic e-commerce platform to a headless architecture to support rapid international expansion, custom storefronts for each market, and faster frontend iteration without backend constraints.
caseStudies.problem: The monolithic platform required full deployments for any frontend change, slowing the design team's ability to iterate. Different markets required localisation features the platform couldn't support without expensive custom development. Mobile performance scores were consistently poor.
caseStudies.solution: A headless architecture was implemented with a React-based storefront consuming the commerce API. Market-specific storefronts were built as separate Next.js applications sharing a component library. Server-side rendering improved performance, and a visual editing layer allowed the marketing team to manage content independently.

results.summary: The headless architecture unlocked a level of frontend agility previously impossible. The marketing team can now launch localised campaigns without engineering involvement, dramatically reducing time-to-market.

caseStudies.title: Inventory Intelligence Platform
caseStudies.description: Demand forecasting to eliminate stockouts and overstock.
caseStudies.overview: A consumer electronics retailer was managing inventory reactively, resulting in costly stockouts during peak demand and excessive overstock of slow-moving products. The goal was to build a demand forecasting system to optimize purchasing decisions.
caseStudies.problem: Buyers relied on gut instinct and basic spreadsheet models to forecast demand. Stockouts during product launches cost an estimated $4M in lost sales annually, while overstock required frequent margin-eroding markdowns to clear warehouse space.
caseStudies.solution: A demand forecasting model was trained on 3 years of sales history, incorporating seasonality, marketing calendar, and external signals such as competitor pricing and search trend data. Automated purchase order recommendations were generated weekly and surfaced to buyers for approval via a decision-support dashboard.

results.summary: Buyers now make purchasing decisions backed by data, with confidence intervals that clearly communicate forecast uncertainty. Warehouse utilisation improved, reducing storage costs alongside the margin gains.

caseStudies.title: Personalised Email Commerce
caseStudies.description: Dynamic product recommendations driving email revenue.
caseStudies.overview: A home goods retailer sent the same promotional emails to its entire subscriber base, achieving industry-average engagement but leaving significant revenue on the table. The goal was to introduce product-level personalisation to increase email-attributed revenue.
caseStudies.problem: Email campaigns featured the same hero products for all subscribers regardless of browse and purchase history. Unsubscribe rates were rising, indicating that irrelevant content was eroding list quality. The email team lacked the tooling to personalise at scale.
caseStudies.solution: A recommendation engine was built on top of customer behaviour data, generating individualised product recommendations for each subscriber at send time. Dynamic content blocks in the email template were populated automatically, and send-time optimisation ensured each subscriber received emails when they were most likely to engage.

results.summary: Personalised email has become the highest-ROI marketing channel for the retailer. List health has improved as subscribers receive relevant content, protecting long-term deliverability.
